items.mistake: Using label/ordinal integers for nominal categories in linear or logistic regression
items.fix: One-hot encode nominal columns (or use regularized target encoding for high cardinality with proper CV). Reserve ordinal maps for truly ordered levels.
items.why: Arbitrary integers impose a false metric: the model treats a one-step increase as a fixed effect even when no order exists.
items.example: Mapping payment_method {cash=0, card=1, crypto=2} makes logistic regression behave as if crypto is "twice" card in log-odds space.

items.mistake: Computing target encoding with the full training set mean per category including the current row
items.fix: Use out-of-fold or leave-one-out means on the training split only; add smoothing toward the global mean for rare categories.
items.why: The feature becomes a direct function of the label, leaking target information and inflating validation metrics.
items.example: `df['city_mean_price'] = df.groupby('city')['price'].transform('mean')` on the same rows used to train a price model.

items.mistake: Fitting OneHotEncoder on train+validation+test before splitting
items.fix: Fit category vocabulary on training data only; transform validation and test with the same learned columns and handle unknowns explicitly.
items.why: Test-set categories influence which dummy columns exist—another form of preprocessing leakage.
items.example: A rare `country=ZZ` appearing only in test creates a column the model never saw during training if vocab is built on all data together incorrectly.

items.fix: Drop raw IDs, use target/frequency encoding with smoothing, hashing, or embeddings; group rare levels into `other`.
items.why: Sparse 50k columns dominate memory, slow training, and memorize individual entities instead of generalizing.
items.example: Customer_id one-hot on 2M rows × 50k columns exhausts RAM; target-encoded purchase rate per id with strong smoothing generalizes better.
